author: misilva73
content: I need to stress that this analysis was based on a block gas limit of 60M gas. We are planning to raise this in Glamsterdam

author: misilva73
content: I think we should re-do this analysis with BAL optimizations in place and assuming higher block limits to be sure that 64kb is safe

author: funnygiulio
content: <@968526635735326741> can you give maybe a gas limit target?

author: misilva73
content: This still needs to be discussed in ACD, but my take would be to target 100Million gas second including BAL optimizations. This would allow us to go to 300M blocks assuming a 3s execution window (which also depends on ePBS)

author: funnygiulio
content: ok, i see

author: funnygiulio
content: so 300M block's gas needs to be processable in 3s

author: funnygiulio

author: misilva73

author: funnygiulio
content: ye, corrected the typo

author: misilva73
content: My understanding is that a 50M block took 1006ms for 64kb, or am I reading that incorrectly?

author: cperezz.eth
content:
Various things. 

- Benchmarks were taken without cache (disabled). This means they have no working pre-fetcher nor can do any optimization whatsoever. Also BALs aren't active, so it's even worse.
- They are **THE WORST POSSIBLE CASE**. Thus, we should expect this to be extremely rare/impossible. The cost of setting this up is insane. And on top of that you need a whole new setup for every "worst case".
- **18,106 EXTCODESIZE operations per block (all different bytecode contracts)**. Don't even understand how this worst case with no cache is taken as "concerning".

author: cperezz.eth
content: Anyways, once we migrate bloatnet to Glamsterdam (please let's make this happen ASAP) we can deploy the attack there and measure the results

author: funnygiulio
content: yeah with BAL the worst case is the best case

author: cperezz.eth
content: I'm not saying BALs just allow 256kB. But, they should def help

author: funnygiulio
content: no no, I think 64KB is more than enough

author: funnygiulio
content: but you need to consider the cache case with BALs. its the whole point of BAL after all

author: funnygiulio
content: so actually this is covered by the analysis

author: misilva73
content: Right, you would have to deploy 18,106 new contracts of that size before running this attack, which would be expensive in practice. However, I still think it would be nice to run this analysis in a closer scenario to glamsterdam (i.e., including BAL optimizations and higher block limits)

author: misilva73
content:
>but you need to consider the cache case with BALs. its the whole point of BAL after all 
>so actually this is covered by the analysis

What do you mean, <@687974325072035882> ? How is it covered?

author: funnygiulio
content: the whole point of BAL is that since you know access beforehand, you can just cache (as you warm them) async

author: funnygiulio
content: or put them in MMAP or whatever cache the DB of the client has

author: funnygiulio
content: its covered as in, it is the "warm cache" timings

author: misilva73
content: I would say the warm case is more representative  of accesses to the same bytecode. If you are loading 18k new contracts, even with pre-fetching, I would expect that performance to be lower, no?

author: funnygiulio
content: no, you can max out the read bandwidth. then you need to redeploy

author: cperezz.eth
content: In here notice we had all the 18k different bytecodes in cache (or almost all of them)

author: cperezz.eth
content: all of them 64kb

author: cperezz.eth
content:
This is why is that fast (we never hit disk (or almost never))

But TBF, BALs won't reach this lvl of perf. Is impossible to be as fast as cache even if you pre-load stuff in background. Anyways, I agree we should see MUCH better numbers

author: funnygiulio
content: yeah this is upper bound perf

author: funnygiulio
content: but again, the attack would be 18k contracts, you slow down a block and then 18k contracts useless because they are now cached

author: misilva73
content: EXTCODESIZE is already included in my state access repricing. It uses that `test_account_access` test, <@703742742135570499> . I think it targets a specific set of contract from bloatnet, so maybe we could point it to the 64kb contracts you already bloated?

author: cperezz.eth
content:
We can't deploy them. If you look at the benchmark, it only goes up to 32. That is because bloatnet would need to "fork" and accept the deployment of bigger contracts for all clients. 

Hence why isn't doable. We can do it for 32 and 100M and estimate 64

author: cperezz.eth
content: Also notice bloating accounts/state here is useless. Only bytecode size matters. So that'sw why I tested with my local node and a modified version of Geth

author: cperezz.eth
content: (I can still run it, I have my old modified DB)

author: cperezz.eth
content: SO I can run the bench for 100M gas if you want
